comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is here and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my sixth-seventh published book, and like everything else I've done for this particular publisher -- Union Square, formerly Sterling -- it wasn't my idea. They contacted me and asked for an introductory book on Druid nature spirituality. I can write one of those more or less in my sleep, so of course I agreed, not least because they're large enough to offer robust advances. It's not part of any series of books, nor does it teach the curriculum of any Druid order; it's a user-friendly, lavishly illustrated little hardback that teaches the complete beginner how to take up the practice of Druidry. Sales have been quite respectable, too. If you're interested, it can be purchased here in the US and at your favorite online or brick-and-mortar bookstore elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no more comments will be put through. See you in a week!***

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List

comments.title: Etheric Energy
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer!

Hope your evening is going well.
I found myself this evening doing some personal experimentation with etheric energy.

I had previously decided that my own etheric energy can expand into the environment; where I suspect it interacts with that energy of other life forms. I have a few questions for your formidable experience:

1. Is etheric energy, in your system’s knowledge, a type of animal magnetism?
2. Is it possible — as I imagined — to perhaps absorb the etheric energy that exists separately on its own in the environment?
3. Is there a relation of a group’s etheric energies to the (I forget the proper term) psychological makeup of the group?

Thanks so much for helping me out!

replies.title: Re: Etheric Energy
replies.content:
1) "Animal magnetism" was Mesmer's term for etheric energy.

2) You do it all the time, with every breath. If you weren't taking in the life force from the environment you'd die very quickly.

3) Yes, of course.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Energy
replies.content:
> 2) You do it all the time, with every breath. If you weren't taking in the life force from the environment you'd die very quickly.

This makes me wonder if the entire idea of 'oxygen' in modern science is just a broken (or very incomplete model) of energy flows as described many other ways. I mean, even in the reductive sense that is what oxygen exchange and reactions literally are describing, but being reductive they might be technically correct and simultaneously completely miss the point.

It is suspicious that so many traditional models are 'air' like. Hmmmmmm.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Energy
replies.content: I'd say it's an incomplete model rather than a broken one. On the material plane, our bodies need oxygen; on the etheric plane, our bodies need certain modes and qualities of ether. Both have to be understood to make sense of life.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Energy
replies.content: Yes, incomplete but also broken in the sense that in knowing the oxygen model potentially excludes other options, or at least significantly obscures them.  What struck me is the idea that there is a lot more going on even on the material plane that what the traditional model of oxygen allows for, and thus what other interesting routes one could study or measure what is typically labelled as etheric phenomena (which is typically approached from an electromagnetic perspective).

replies.title: Re: Etheric Energy
replies.content:
Nope, please let me disagree: you stop breathing, you die sooner or later; and no amount of magic is going to change that.

The teachings I learned from Traditional Chinese Medicine, which themselves come from Daoism, say that the Qi of Air meets in the Spleen with the Qi of Rice, and those are transformed into True Qi and sustain the life in the body. The way the process is described sounds remarkably close to cellular respiration and ATP: the main difference being the anatomic proximity to the solar plexus chakra (instead of through the whole body). My take is that "oxygen" is the material manifestation of a spiritual reality that also has a presence in the etheric plane, which the Chinese philosophers were sage enough to identify as "Air Qi" instead of the more generic "Qi" label. Ditto for the "glucose" vs "Qi of Rice" duality.

I’d intended to ask why a pentagram is inscribed in each element’s corner for the LBRP, but I came across an explanation in the sacred geometry section of CGD Bardic grade (which was fascinating) that the 1:phi ratio “represents regeneration and the balanced union of the powers of the masculine and the feminine. comments.title: Arthur C. Clarke
comments.content:
(I’d appreciate comments from the commentariat if JMG isn’t aware of any occult influences in Arthur C. Clarke’s writings)

Hi JMG,

Are you aware of any occult influences in Arthur C. Clarke’s writings?

I may be reading too much into a chapter towards the end of the novel “2001: A Space Odyssey,” but it struck me over the weekend as I was listening to the audiobook version that the main character David Bowman’s transformation from human to Star-Child felt similar to comments you have posted regarding the afterlife and "The Cosmic Doctrine."

If I remember correctly, you have stated that after death, we will process our memories in reverse order.

As someone poorly educated in the occult, I found that reverse-order feature odd, and it struck me as more than a coincidence that Clarke’s Bowman likewise processes his memories in reverse order during his transformation.

Although the reverse-order feature struck me the most, the entire chapter describing his transformation felt like it echoed your writings about the occult, particularly your Book Club posts about "The Cosmic Doctrine."

Again, I acknowledge that I may be reading too much into something that is nothing.

Thanks,
-Eugene

P.S. Before submitting this question, I became aware of the 1985 British television show “Arthur C. Clarke’s World of Strange Powers” via internet search results. By the reviews, I can’t tell if the show was a hatchet job, or if it tried to give a fair shake to the "paranormal" … I assume the former.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: I've never heard that Clarke had any direct exposure to occultism. On the other hand, one of the things that today's SF authors and pundits do their best to ignore and erase is the huge role that occultism had in SF before the rationalist witch hunts of the 1980s. A great many SF and fantasy authors were up to their eyeballs in occultism, and put huge amounts of it into their fiction. Since Clarke, like most of the writers of SF's great era, read SF voraciously, he'll have absorbed a great deal of occultism at second hand.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
Slightly OT mini-rant:

Scooby Doo was a conspiracy on the part of Hanna Barbara to indoctrinate the young with pseudoskepticism. No monster or ghost is real. They are all crooks on a scheme.

At least that is my interpretation!

It would have been much more interesting for a cartoon gang of kids and their dog to be real paranormal investigators!

Take that Casey Kasem!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AVroc7RvNeU&ab_channel=forcingmetochooseaname

JPM

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: I'd have a hard time disagreeing with that. Still, there's a flip side to the coin, which is that Hanna-Barbera also did an equivalent series in which the monsters were real, The Funky Phantom. Its ratings were so low that it only lasted one season. Keep in mind that pop culture and deliberate propaganda hav a complex relationship -- propaganda shapes pop culture, but pop culture also defines which propaganda campaigns succeed and which don't.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
Thanks for this John! I will have to ask my friend Syd about the Funky Phantom. I never heard of it. He is my go to source for all things horror and video and pop culture related with an intense encyclopedic knowledge. I'll probably get a dissertation out of him!

As for the relationship between propaganda and pop culture, I will definitely be keeping that in mind when I look into the ways they feed into each other.

I mostly meant that comment as a lark, but I have thought of writing a subgenii style rant about the topic, whether it is true or not. Thanks for helping me get warmed up!

JPM

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: "Pardon my language. But YEEEEEHAW, let the sons of God and man bear witness!"

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
Don't tell Connie!

JPM

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
Something very odd just clicked: A few years ago I looked into how the Nielson Ratings are determined. I am far from sure that the TV ratings have much if any relationship with reality, given how many spots there are for someone who wants to try to game the ratings to do so. However, I am not sure it would have been possible to do this before the corporate computer revolution in the late 1960s and early 1970s: it could, theoretically be done, but in practice without computers it would have required far too much computation and probably would never be practical: anyone who could afford those resources would probably have other less intense ways to attain their goals.

Western culture started going off the rails shortly thereafter, and a lot of people started seeing through the propoganda campaigns (even as they continued to run, and the poltical and elite classes continued to be affected). It actually makes perfect sense that the PR industry would go down this road: once the metrics used to measure the success of the PR campaigns could be manipulated, they would be for the simple reason that figuring out ways of manipulating metrics is a large part of what the PR industry does. Once this happened, their ability to gauge how effective their campaigns were would become increasingly dysfunctional, but this could take a long time for anyone to notice, especially since the PR system would still be able to make it look like everything was working fine to anyone who looked at the metrics and didn't look too closely, and most of the comfortable classes would play along because to do otherwise would mean losing their perks and privileges....

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: That's fascinating. What you're suggesting, if I understand correctly, is that PR started losing effectiveness because they were gaming metrics instead of doing effective propaganda. Yes, that would explain a lot...

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
I just saw that I was not signed in for the above, and would like to apologize for that.

That is exactly what I'm suggesting, and I'm suggesting it's related to the computer age making it easier to game metrics. On further thought, I don't see any reason to think it is soley a PR industry problem: computers, by making it much, much easier to do calculations and track massive data sets, make it a lot easier to game almost any metric, which means a computerized society, all else being equal, will always have far more cases of gaming metrics.

Another example of this might be p-hacking: it is relatively tedious to check a hundred different combination of variables by hand, but with a computer it's trivial. As a result, I'd be willing to wager that p-hacking only emerged in a large way once statistical software emerged; but then, thanks to the Curse of Dimensionality (look at enough relationships and something will always be significant through chance), it has proceeded to ruin science.

This is probably worth a lot more thought, because it brings a lot of otherwise unrelated things together.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: Your comment about p-hacking is quite correct. When I was at college the first time in the early 1980s, I knew people who were helping to invent p-hacking using the mainframe computers at the university I attended. What I heard is that by hand, it was so labor-intensive that nobody could afford the cost without triggering an audit by the university, but that with a computer it was so fast and inexpensive that researchers could easily hide the modest cost in their research budgets.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
Sounds like you've independently re-discovered "Goodhardt's Law," which is usually colloquially rendered "any measure that becomes a target ceases to be a good measure." Anything meant to be an outside, objective measure of "how are we doing?" that folks start being evaluated on creates immense pressure for them to find any and every way to game it. GDP comes to mind as one of the more egregious examples.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: I think it's a little more subtle than that, and I wish I had remembered to mention Goodhardt's Law. What I'm getting at is that there's a case for certain metrics working well and being impervious to Goodhardting, under the conditions that existed before the computer age, but not since. Trying to game the metrics associated with the Nielson Rating's could be done without computers, but it would be far more expensive and far more challenging; as well as more likely to get caught. With computers, some manipulation of the ratings is probably in reach of any major broadcaster or production studio; and I would expect a lot of the metrics used to track how well PR is working are similar.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
Fair enough - I'd certainly agree that the tendency to Goodhardt's Law and the capacity to actually follow it might often be somewhat separable - in an analogous way to how people have always had a tendency to want to destroy their enemies, but planes, rockets, and nukes give them the ability to do it in bigger and more horrible ways than ever before.

What strikes me as likely significant about your observation about computing power and metric specifically is a) that the stated purpose of the computing power isn't as obviously/intrinsically linked with the risk it creates, as, say, deadlier weapons are, and b) the change in capacity introduced by computing power can transform something that was once helpful into something actively harmful (here, a better analogy than weapons might be food - our propensity to eat as much sugar and fat as we can get our hands on was great in an environment where those resources were fairly scarce, but can make us unhealthy where there's more than enough).

At any rate, interesting line of speculation, thank you for sharing it!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
What I find the most interesting is that computing power is often represented as allowing people to find when someone is tampering with the data. By gathering everything and allowing people to see everything, it supposdely allows for people to see connections which might otherwise be missed; and the massive computational power allows for people to run their own analyses and so catch fraud and distortions by others.

The first immediately ties to the Curse of Dimensionality, while the second only works if people have sufficient intelligence to run their own analyses, which I think is quite often false. So the entire narative that justifies the computer age is most likely not just wrong, but actually the opposite of reality...

JMG, would this suggest to you that computers are Plutonian? If so, does that imply that the computer age will end by 2036? The transformation of society that that would entail seems so wrenching and disruptive I find it hard to figure out a plausible way for that to happen...

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: I've tended to see computers as Uranian rather than Plutonian, but I could be wrong...

replies.title: Scooooooby Doo!
replies.content:
It’s funny how fast they started to subvert the ghosts being fake early on, hints in early episodes of stuff the Crooks couldn’t do and in the 70s series (the one that opens with a giant pair of eyes looking down at Scooby then skipping to him water sking) they had a working spell book.

Until in the 80s films and Scrapy episodes you had full on monsters.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content: There have been more recent Scooby Doo releases in which the monsters ARE in fact real. That doesn't help those of us who grew up with the crooks in costumes, of course.

replies.title: Re: Arthur C. Clarke
replies.content:
Not JMG, but I've noticed this as well - there's an openness to the unexplained in Clarke that you don't see in, say, Asimov. Not only Bowman's transformation as noted by OP, but the way Morgan's luck runs out when he loses his talisman (the spinnerette) and the way the monks leave the mountaintop after the butterflies are blown there in "The Fountains of Paradise." And there's the theme in "Childhood's End" that technology is a dead end and if humanity is to change, well, humanity has to change, and into something beyond our comprehension!

-Roldy-

comments.title: Blessed are you, Venus-Cloacina, lady of the sewage
comments.content:
You had mentioned before that it would be welcome to post our thanks to patron saints, goddesses and gods. I thought that if I was going to do that, it would be nice to share some of the story behind it.

I have been doing work with Venus for sometime now, but mostly through prayer. It has really been me the one that has been taking more out of the relationship so I started to grow a little uncomfortable of not giving something back besides prayer time. It occurred to me thanks to your comment, as I posted here some months ago, that a good offering to Venus would be to her aspect as Venus Cloacina, goddess of the Cloaca Maxima in the Roman Empire --their main drainage and sewage system that kept the city clean.

One way to do it today I thought, was to pick up trash from the streets, given that there is no shortage of that. So I was in the middle of calculating costs for supplies, taking longer that needed and overdoing it as usual when in one of my walks I encountered someone picking up trash from the streets with a vest and something that looked like a logo on the trash bags. I stopped her and asked what was she doing. After a quick chat on how ugly things looked in that specific corner, she directed me to the city adopt-a-street program. Ok... I will take that as a: "Yes, I am interested and stop looking for excuses, here you go, free supplies. How many bags would you like?" I came out of an industrial district warehouse with two tote bags choke-full with heavy duty bags, graffiti remover, gloves, safety vests and all sorts of the good stuff. When I was leaving, the volunteer sent me on my way not without mentioning: I had to come and say hi personally, I loved your username. I had chosen the username to be Venus The Cleanser, and she knew about her. Hah! I decided no more questions were needed.

I proceeded to have quite the quest, you really can get to know your city this way, and good gods are there stories in the trash.

Did you know that small grafiti with a good sense of line make good sigils to bless trash containers? I promise it was her idea!

Thank you Venus-Cloacina, for the lessons on the hard realities of the lack of harmony in the public spaces, giving me a sense of how things should flow; the humbleness and the laughs. May your purifying touch spread through the neglected spaces of human indifference.

--Venus The Cleanser

A good start to the week, JMG and commenters!

When we incarnate here, we already come with a “programming” established in the natal chart by the “engineers of incarnation.” Regardless of our religious system or beliefs, we necessarily have to go through what is described in the birth chart, correct? Is there any idea of who these “engineers of incarnation” might be?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Er, "programming"? "Engineers of incarnation"? Those seem like very awkward metaphors to me.

Each soul, once it rises above the animal level and is no longer guided by the group soul of the species in which it is incarnated, has a personal spiritual guide emanated from the divine -- "guardian angel" is one traditional word for this entity. That entity works with the slowly awakening higher self of the individual to help it incarnate in situations that will help it clear its karma and prepare for a life outside of material incarnation. Over time, the higher self takes on more and more of this work.

As for the natal chart, it inclines, it does not compel. Your beliefs or religious system don't have any influence on how you respond to your natal chart, however -- that depends on your qualities of character and your level of consciousness. At the higher levels of incarnate human existence, the influence of the natal chart is more of a toolkit than a set of preestablished events.

comments.title: Chronically Disturbed Dreamer
comments.content:
Good day to you! I am a frequent reader but rare contributor, so first as always: Thank you for providing this venue for unusual ideas and inquiries.

My question is about dreams: not the specific content, but the mode of experiencing them. I have slowly come to suspect, over the years, that I may not experience them in quite the normal way. Of course I could be wrong, but it seems to me that most people describe their dreaming experience as immersive and natural -- that no matter how weird the dream content is, the weirdness does not register during the dreaming experience itself.

This is not the case for me. I am not a lucid dreamer; I don’t consciously realize that I am dreaming, let alone control the dream. And yet, there is almost always a strong sense of bemusement or even bafflement, no matter what is seemingly occurring – a usually vague but insistent impression that *something is wrong here*, and I need to get away from it. My dreaming self does generally go with the flow of the dream, but feels uncomfortable and out of step in doing so. This experience is long-term, persistent, and pervasive; I can count the truly personal and meaningful dreams that I recall having with fingers left over.

As a result, I have long mused over your teaching that not all of what presents itself in our dreamscapes is personal material – that it is not unusual for oddments from the wider astral realm to insinuate themselves. And I recently found myself wondering if that is not the issue for me. Could it be that I am picking up on the presence of “foreign” material? Though my dreaming mind tries to cover it with a veneer of familiar people, places, and situations, the fit is forced and the seams show; perhaps on some level my dreaming self recognizes and is disturbed by the discrepancies.

Does this seem plausible to you? And if so, does the experience indicate anything relevant about my spiritual condition or development? Is there something particular that I can do in light of it to help my mental sheath along? And of course, if this experience is actually quite common and unremarkable, that would be good to know too, lol. (For reference, I am not a magician, but I study and contemplate occult philosophy quite seriously, have a committed practice of meditative prayer, and try my best to apply the fruits of both to my daily life.)

replies.title: Re: Chronically Disturbed Dreamer
replies.content: Hmm! That's very interesting. Do you often have the same sense of bemusement or bafflement in any parts of your waking life?

replies.title: Re: Chronically Disturbed Dreamer
replies.content:
OP here. I do have chronic anxiety that makes me feel easily overwhelmed in everyday situations, but it's not at all the same kind of funhouse mirror-world impression.

More similar would be the times when I've gotten too far into my own head: I've been studying or contemplating intensely, and more or less suddenly it's like everything just stops making abstract sense -- not just the topic of study, but reality in general. Very weird and quite disturbing, but stepping back and grounding myself in more embodied pursuits and simple prayer for the rest of the day tends to mend it.

No, I can't say that I ever feel in waking life the same impression of *wrongness* that I generally experience in my dreams. Which is for the best, because if I did feel that way, I would probably begin to fear that I was having some sort of mental break from reality!

replies.title: Re: Chronically Disturbed Dreamer
replies.content: Thank you. These things being the case, yes, I suspect that you're more than usually sensitive to the fact that dream-imagery comes from outside yourself.

replies.title: Re: Chronically Disturbed Dreamer
replies.content:
Thank you, that's reassuring. Is it a sensitivity with any other useful applications, do you think, or is it just one of those sensory quirks that people sometimes come with?

I have to admit that it isn't particularly pleasant; it means that I can't really relax and enjoy my dream life, and that in itself seems to impair restfulness a bit. Do you think that attempting to learn lucid dreaming techniques might be of any use in alleviating the negative effects?

replies.title: Re: Chronically Disturbed Dreamer
replies.content: Both of those are fine questions to which I don't know the answers. It would certainly be worth experimenting with both of them!

replies.title: Re: Chronically Disturbed Dreamer
replies.content: Very fair! :)

comments.title: Ether
comments.content:
Greetings JMG! I have few questions.

1) An energy healer I am working with offhandedly said that qi/ether was electromagnetic. I was surprised because I considered it to be a completely different substance. What could you share about the nature of ether and how it connects to the mental and physical planes?

2) You'd said that ether flows along the nervous system and some neurons are able to connect to it. Is this all or does ether also have a field from which it directly works between the mental and the physical plane, say directly to organs, cells etc (related to what I am sharing below)?

Perhaps you can help me with this: in Chinese medicine they don't really talk about the nervous system or nerves at all, but say that qi moves the blood and provide energy to the muscles. All the important stuff is at the level of qi, and the physical plane is more passive /yin.

Now if we are to think of both qi and nerves as real (as they are), is qi upstream of the nervous system? ie it affects the electrochemical system that regulates the nervous system?

As an example, if someone suffers severely mental or physical trauma that causes blood circulation and muscular issues on the physical plane, how is that stored as "memory" along the planes?

My guess was that the trauma is often stored on the astral levels, along with etheric levels, and because it constantly replays in the background (subconscious) of the mind, it disrupts the flow of qi in the energy body which causes the sympathetic nervous system to activate for extended periods and cause blood flow to slow down.


3) Where do elementals fit in and what is their role?


4) Have you heard of Michael Levin at Tufts who is working on bioelectricity and morphogenesis? He seems to be doing the work to provide formalisation of energy body phenomena and particularly of the interactional dualism framework (tbh its more than 2 substances).

Some references/links for those curious:

"The whole body is a transduction system converting cognitive content into chemistry — We are psychobiological information processing systems"
https://x.com/MitoPsychoBio/status/1887445382227017783

"What Bodies Think About: Bioelectric Computation Outside the Nervous System"
"Bodies have bioelectrical patterns that store information "memories" separately from genomic, anatomical states. These bioelectrical patterns play a huge role in developmental processes, so that being able to control them is basically like a holy grail of regenerative medicine. It also offers interesting new insights for AI and cognition."
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RjD1aLm4Thg


"Where Minds Come From: the scaling of collective intelligence"
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=44W9Mw4AGT8

"“What if medicine could harness the body’s innate healing ability with precision, using technology to direct the body to repair damaged tissues and organs, or even regenerate them entirely?”
https://t.co/2StfGcwA1C

replies.title: Re: Ether
replies.content:
1) The etheric realm has a complex relationship to electromagnetism, which hasn't been explored in adequate detail. Until we get a better understanding of the etheric, questions like this will have no good answers.

2) The etheric body penetrates and acts through every cell in the material body, including neurons. The etheric body also has its own organs, channels, and structures distinct from the material body. It's not a simple matter!

3) That's a good subject for an entire book. Fit into what? Their role in what?

4) No, I wasn't aware of him. Thanks for the heads up.

comments.title: Planetary Charity and Electional Questions
comments.content:
I'm in a situation where I need to hire a new lawyer to correct mistakes my now-deceased lawyer made when setting up the legal framework for a business I run. I only learned of these mistakes after he died when I was searching for a new attorney and more than one that I interviewed said something to the effect of "how this was done isn't right". Although I'm dismayed at this, I obviously need to get it fixed. My main concerns with hiring a new lawyer are that he does fix things properly and that I don't incur any more costs than needed since I already paid the first lawyer for his (unknown to me) shoddy work. I also hope to mitigate/avoid any potential ramifications from the poor work of the first lawyer.

1) Would it be Mercury and/or Jupiter that I should appeal to for success in this endeavor?

2) What would I look for in an electional chart to choose a good time to retain this new lawyer?

Thanks,

FSH

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity and Electional Questions
replies.content:
1) I'd choose Jupiter for this.

2) Depends on your natal chart. If Jupiter's dignified or neutral in your natal chart, make sure that he's at least neutral in the electional chart and that the Moon is applying to him by trine, sextile, or conjunction. If he's debilitated in your natal chart, choose a different planet and try to elect a good time for that planet.

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity and Electional Questions
replies.content:
Thank you. Jupiter is domiciled in Pisces in my natal chart and is my most dignified planet, so I'll go with Jupiter. Thanks again!

FSH

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity and Electional Questions
replies.content: In that case, get Jupiter decently dignified and with the Moon applying by favorable aspect, and you should have no trouble getting good results.

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity and Electional Questions
replies.content:
Well, I can't wait until Jupiter moves into Cancer in June so it will be in detriment in Gemini, but my natal Moon is in Aquarius so would it work to choose a day when the Moon is in Aquarius and is in trine to Jupiter?

Thanks,

FSH

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity and Electional Questions
replies.content: Time it with Jupiter rising or conjunct the midheaven and, so long as Jupiter isn't involved in applying negative aspects, and the Moon's applying favorably, you should be good.

replies.title: Re: Planetary Charity and Electional Questions
replies.content:
Thank you for your assistance with this.

FSH

comments.title: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
comments.content:
I hit a few snags in my understanding of The Cosmic Doctrine.

1. What planes are each swarm currently on? For example, I think we, the 4th swarm, are on the 7th, 6th, and 5th, and developing a mental body on the 4th. The Lords of Mind have three bodies, which planes are working on right now? How about the Lords of Form and Flame? I assume the latter is on the 1st, only.

2. I understand each planet has a keynote based on what body the Lords of Flame were forming when it originated. So they made a 7th plane planet, then moved on and made a 6th plane planet, then a 5th, etc., and those planets will fill in the other bodies around those keynotes. But let's take the planet formed with a keynote of the 5th plane. Then the Lords of Form show up with a 6th plane body and working on the 5th. There is still no 7th plane contact. Does that planet have to wait for the Lords of Mind to show up with three bodies, 7th, 6th, and 5th, before it gets its 7th plane body?

3. If so, that means the 1st plane keynote planet is still lacking a number of bodies, and until the 7th swarm completes its trip, there will be at least some planets lacking a body or two on a certain plane?

4. Or does, say, the Lord of Flames split out into 7 subgroups and each one works on a planet at the same time, with each subsequent swarm also dividing itself between the planets, so we don't really move planets, but the bodies of the planet increase?

It might be easier to answer all these at once if you have a handy chart of which swarms are on what planet/plane, and their route of travel. I do understand this is not literal and we don't mean the actual planets, but even in the metaphorical system I'm having problems seeing how the swarms move.

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
replies.content:
1) No, our swarm is entirely on the 7th, and we'll remain here until all of us have evolved past the need for a 7th plane body.

2) No, they built all the bodies of each planet while they were there. It's just that one of them, the one corresponding to the bodies the Lords of Flame built for themselves, has a special imprint.

3 & 4) No and no.

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
replies.content: Makes sense, thank you. If we are completely on the 7th, which plane are the Lords of Flame, Form, and Mind currently on, respectively?

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
replies.content: My understanding is that they've completed the whole circuit. Fortune apparently believed that there were many other swarms between them and us.

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
replies.content: Last week you noted the three big swarms as being responsible for creating the solar system, chemical reactions, and simple life. So I can see that the physical solar system will remain after the Lords of Force move on. But we still have bacteria, which had been the third swarm, and something still has to be animating those, right? Fortune claimed that simpler organisms like bacteria persisting in the world were slow-moving sparks from past swarms that eventually would go extinct here. But she didn't know that symbiotic bacteria are absolutely essential to all us complex life forms. Do you think that bacteria are driven by leftovers from an earlier swarm, or continue to be animated by the oversight of the third swarm even though they themselves are long gone from this plane?

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
replies.content: The work of the first three swarms continues after they've moved on. As I see it, bacteria are in the process of synthesizing into dispersed collective life forms; by the time our swarm has completed its work, that process will be complete, and the bacterial sphere will be a single collective life form of which the planetary intelligence of the Earth will be the guiding oversoul. replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
replies.content:
You are pretty accurate as far as I have seen in my own work with bacteria, regarding being a collective life form.

Although technically a single cell of bacteria is a unit of the species, in my opinion, the correct way of looking at prokaryotes like bacteria is to consider the entire strain of a specific genome as an "individual" of the bacteria species.

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine Progression of Swarms Through Planets
replies.content: Back when I was in college the first time, I considered going into biology, and making a career researching the ecosystems of the forest understory, with a focus on bryophytes. The main reason I didn't go that route is that jobs in the field were becoming scarce, and the professors advised me against it. In the process, though, I learned a lot of systems ecology, and came to realize that an organism is basically an ecosystem that's a little more compact than most; from there to the idea of dispersed prokaryotic organisms wasn't much of a leap.

comments.title: Lords of Flame
comments.content:
Greetings JMG.

My recent meditations on the topic of the Lords of Flames feel like I am staring at a vast chasm in the sense that I'm not sure how to even start navigating these ideas.

If the Lords of Flame never had any bodies (defined in this case as an interface allowing a soul to perceive and interact with its environment), then I fail to grasp how they managed to learn or accomplish anything such as the shaping of our physical solar system. Conversely, the involvement of bodies with the Lords of Form and Mind makes meditation on these Lords more accessible to me.

I can grasp the notion of a Divine Spark shaping some physical substance to form a physical body to allow for specialized involvement with the physical plane, but is it possible for an unenbodied Spark to extensively mould and shape substance within a plane? I feel like I could meditate deeper on the Lords of Flame if I knew this, but ultimately, not knowing the answer to something that presumably occured billions of years ago is not going to hinder my work in this life, though I am curious.

Thanks as always for hosting MM.

JR

replies.title: Re: Lords of Flame
replies.content: A body is an enduring interface allowing a soul to perceive and interact with its environment. The Lords of Flame could temporarily "possess" substances and direct them, but the connection dissolved as soon as a Lord of Flame turned its attention to the next task. It was just as necessary for substances to get used to being vehicles for consciousness as it was for conscious sparks to evolve the ability to enter into substantial forms; the Lords of Flame accomplished both those tasks, and made it possible for the Lords of Form to create the first enduring bodies for souls thereafter.

replies.title: Re: Lords of Flame
replies.content: Makes sense. Thank you for your time and have a great Monday.

replies.title: Re: Lords of Flame
replies.content:
A follow-up question.

As the Lords of Flame evolved up the planes, I suppose they also habituated the substances of those higher planes to being vehicles for consciousness, allowing subsequent swarms to take on etheric bodies, astral bodies, mental bodies, etc. Is this correct?

JR

replies.title: Re: Lords of Flame
replies.content: That's correct. Then the Lords of Form and Mind added their habituations, which is why we can hold together bodies on three planes at a time.

replies.title: Re: Lords of Flame
replies.content:

If so, in one sense, lucky them! I'm already envious that they will be able to access the full mental world as easily as we daydream while still physically embodied. But they also get to keep a permanent, individualized presence in the astral?

On the other hand: lucky us! I figure the more permanent bodies you accumulate, the more limited your expression is (although, as a wise man once said, limits are power).

Whereas we will eventually be able to pop in and out of the astral at will — it just won't be home — once they get there, they'll be clothed in that particular astral body forever. (Hmm… thinking about it, this will make their reincarnation very strange once they develop an astral body but aren't even close to ready to discard the physical body yet. It makes me think that to be reborn for them will be like waking up from a dream.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
On Thursday, February 20th, last week, I kept finding myself overcome at random by waves of sadness, despair, and almost grief, without any discernible reason (no such issues in my life, and no hormonal or bodily reason). At several points I nearly found myself crying.

This doesn’t generally happen to me (I am not a moody person) and I found it bizarre. I worried I was having a premonition of some tragedy.

Today I think I found the answer. I live less than 3 miles from the site of the tragic Station Night Club fire in West Warwick, RI (moved here last year from elsewhere in RI) and I read about memorial ceremonies to mark its anniversary today. I guessed immediately that the actual anniversary had been Thursday, and it was. (It happened February 20, 2003.)

I live in an older, working class neighborhood full of people who have lived here for generations. (For example, all our immediate neighbors are RI natives who have lived in our neighborhood all their lives.)

I’m certain now that I was picking up on the general grief of people in my neighborhood. 100 people died and hundreds more were injured, the vast majority of them very local to this area. The first firefighters on scene were from my neighborhood. And so on. Certainly it is the worst event in the history of our community.

This kind of thing happens to me fairly regularly. I could write several more examples. But I’m wondering—do you have any advice on managing this? At times it is debilitating.

I do have a discursive meditation and prayer rule that I follow almost daily but it seems to have heightened these experiences for me. I don’t exactly what to deaden this ability, but maybe channel it better.

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That makes sense. If you know that something like this is coming from outside you, it's possible to "lean" against it, so to speak, and acknowledge it without letting it affect your own state too greatly.

(The 20th was also difficult for me, but for a more personal reason -- that was the first anniversary of my wife's death.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I am very sorry for your loss.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you. It's been a rough year, but the old tradition of a year of mourning has a lot of value; now that it's past, it feels like time to reengage a little more with life.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I was going to post this as its own thread, but maybe this subthread about the dangers of identifying with mythical figures is a better place for it.

The following is probably going to come up during the open thread on Wednesday, and there's some points I wanted to get through ahead of time.

A splinter group out of the Yudkowsky-Alexander rationalist movement/scene, which was ejected from its main formal institutions ca. 2019 but continued to draw from the scene's chatroom social circles and peripheral blog audiences, has recently had its main leader figure and several other members arrested. They are connected with four known violent deaths of others, one violent death from amongst themselves (shot by a victim in self-defense), and one very plausibly ideologically-triggered suicide. (Also one more suicide, that from my limited information was maybe sort of in the cards anyway, but still probably really ought to have been avoided.)

I haven't been following the news very closely, but the following slightly outdated news articles seemed to reflect adequate investigations and get almost all the facts right as far as I can tell:

https://www.sfchronicle.com/bayarea/article/ziz-lasota-zizians-rationalism-20063671.php

https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/german-math-genius-get-drawn-cult-accused-coast-coast-killings-rcna189309

Everyone is calling it a cult, and I guess that's fair at this point even though the group tried to have a non-hierarchical ideology; it's just that one person, Jack/"Ziz" LaSota, had most of the motivating charisma and intellectual firepower and mythcrafting drive, especially after the other main intellectual contributor apparently noped out and went to ground after the katana/shooting incident in 2022.

There are a really quite remarkable number of synchronicities and circumstantial cues around this situation of the sort that one would in retrospect expect if it had been the result of someone involved having had a devotion to Lucifer, although admittedly I do not have a (figurative) smoking gun to present.

1. You've mentioned some of the effects that devotions to different divine figures tend to have on someone's overall vibe. Unfortunately I don't have the links for the remarks in question right now because they are spread across multiple Ecosophia articles and Dreamwidth posts and don't come up on search engines, but I remember it as something like: if someone has a devotion to Loki, they get this sort of sneering adolescent quality behind all their emotional presentation; if they have a devotion to Satan, they get that plus a sort of windbag pomposity; and if they have a devotion to Lucifer, they get the sneering adolescent quality plus a note of a sort of uncomprehending wounded dignity (though I don't think you actually used the word "uncomprehending"; that part I was probably reading between the lines). I had met LaSota on a few occasions before things got bad. (I'm going to go with she/her pronouns for LaSota; she would have had time to legally transition by now if not distracted by her rebellion project.) I remember her having a whiff of the sneering adolescence thing and a bit more of the uncomprehending/oblivious wounded dignity thing, and this was well before all that other stuff went down. (There was also maybe a little bit of pomposity, but maybe I'm just imagining that, and the pomposity wasn't distinctively blustering or as-though-constantly-having-to-feign-disregard-of-an-affront or whatever -- it was maybe more like having a little fun.)

2. While LaSota invented this whole big profusion of mythicized and symbolic language for the psychological and social and moral phenomena that she wanted to talk about, she didn't really have much occasion to discuss religion as such. However, at one point somewhere in her blog she did make an offhanded remark that (paraphrasing from memory to add context) she considered the Abrahamic god to be the one symbol that most epitomized the evil mutually-enforced consensus system pervading modern human civilization that threatened to consume and falsify all human freedom and ability to recognize good. Now, I'm reading between the lines here again, but from what I remember of her, it totally would have been like LaSota to have contemplated "if that's the distinction between the Abrahamic god and devil..." and made some emotionally invested commitment like "...then I'm definitely on the devil's side", and then sort of put that commitment aside thinking it didn't matter, only occasionally reaffirming it when the thought came up again.

3. LaSota's preferred name "Ziz" is a reference to a reference to a creature from Jewish mythology made by the serially published novel "Worm". In that story, "Ziz" was the name authorities assigned to an alien monster who took a seraph-like form of a giant alabaster woman partially covered by large randomly placed and sized wings. The monster would descend upon cities and use its superpowers of telekinesis and foresight to make tiny nudges to the psychology of everyone in the city, to set up dominoes so that, during key moments weeks or years in the future, its victims would suffer precisely-aimed stress freakouts or psychotic delusions, destroying their lives and the lives of those around them and sabotaging institutions that humanity might use to regain its balance against an ongoing alien invasion/training exercise. (The public instead called the monster the Simurgh, because of the auditory hallucinations of screaming that it caused around it.) LaSota somehow didn't regard the maleficence of her chosen namesake as relevant, merely remarking that she liked the feeling of knowingness that she associated with the character.

4. The main starting point of ideological power that LaSota ended up farming, to get a following initially, was a synchronistically-acquired surprising novel way to plausibly support an accusation that almost everyone was secretly not good, and to find two putative victim populations to rebel against the general consensus of society on behalf of (nonhuman animals and some peoples' naturally-benevolent sides). Her first major (unconscious) power play also involved some synchronicities that helped her find an accusation to rebel against the institutions of the rationality movement on the basis of. (Much of that accusation seems to have been false, and almost all of the rest of it seems to have been greatly trumped-up, though not all of the errors could have easily been deduced by them as a significant possibility at the time.) I bring up the parts about accusation because the name "Satan" derives from one of the characters in the book of Job, named for his divine court office of finding accusations to make against people (which in the story turned out to be plausible but false); and of course Lucifer is known for rebellion.

5. LaSota had some kind of strong urge to identify with supervillains. E.g. from the NBC article: "'Sometimes cops harass me for wearing my religious attire as a Sith,' LaSota wrote on her blog. '(As a Sith, I’m religiously required to do whatever I want, and for now that so happens to include wearing black robes).'" She had a strangely strong animus against Anna Salamon, the woman who had in 2017 (?) shut down LaSota's case for being given a position of influence in the two central organizations of the rationality movement. I think the way it went was like, Salamon had based her decision on being uneasy with the unsettling features of the whole picture that LaSota presented, but the supervillain imagery kind of made an anchor point to hang all the other reasons for misgivings around. Then someone informed LaSota about how Salamon's decision was significantly influenced by the supervillain imagery. Reading between the lines again, it seems like LaSota's animus derived from reacting to this personal and institutional rejection like something in between a transgender person having their gender invalidated and a narcissist having their self-image refused.

6. After what down with their protest in 2019, they were assigned a public defender. He quit the case (maybe because they were unwilling to work with him because he wasn't vegan?) and afterwards spontaneously described LaSota as "the antichrist".

7. Someone a continent away, who so far as I understand was not particularly involved, had a psychotic episode where he had a delusional conviction that LaSota wanted to enslave and torture everyone forever. (I think this was on LaSota's blog?) These are of course projects commonly attributed to the devil.

8. A bit before all of that, according to news reports, LaSota had published a card game titled "Dystheism" for a competition. (From what I read in the news, the card game was about trying to survive an afterlife controlled by malevolent powers.)

9. JMG has also mentioned that people with devotions to Satan tend to find more and more excuses to justify and try to get away with violations of a group's identifiable boundaries until they've either destroyed the group or gotten "thrown out of heaven". I do know one story of LaSota engaging in a surprising almost unmotivated violation of a privacy-related boundary that would go with this proposed pattern, some time before the whole revolutionary ideology would have really come into focus to properly motivate such a thing as wartime espionage or whatever, but sharing the details might be personally identifying to the person I heard the story from.

10. This is more of a demonic influence in general thing than anything specific to Lucifer, but one of the news articles mentions that the police assigned to investigate the group's trailer homes after the 2022 incident were creeped out and sort of wanted to put on hazmat suits before going in.

The first suicide is also potentially noteworthy, because the group's ideology caused someone to split into factions, and suicide seems to have been the form that the formerly disempowered faction's rebellion against the formerly ruling faction took. That might be more of a Plutonian thing than a Luciferian thing though.

(Now that I think of it, there's another weird partial synchronicity. Gwen Danielson, the one who discovered the evidence leading to the group's beliefs about brain hemispheres, and who seems to have left after the 2022 katana/shooting incident, had a blog titled "The World to Save It". This appears to be a needlessly obfuscated reference to a prophecy in the fantasy series "The Wheel of Time", in which one character is predicted to "give up half the light of the world to save it". Danielson might have meant this to express angst about sacrificing the egocentric goals of one hemisphere for the altruistic goals of the other. In the series, what actually happens is that the character loses one of his eyes, I think in a trade to the fey. Meanwhile, in the 2022 incident, the stabbing victim lost one eye. I'm not sure there's a way to connect any more dots than that, though, since the victim was eventually murdered later to prevent him from testifying, and that doesn't correspond to what happened in the book.)

I do have some questions, but this comment is very long by standards around here already. My main question is: I never managed to find the remark about Luciferian devotion and "wounded dignity" again, after having read it the first time. Did I get it right? Am I confabulating it and it actually came from someone else? (I did find the remarks about the effects of devotions to Loki and Satan; they were in an exchange on the main blog where you successfully guessed that someone had a devotion to Set.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't recall writing that, so it may have come from somebody else. The whole Zizian business is one of those dreary tragedies in which a bunch of people go careening down an overfamiliar path to disaster. For example, "non-hierarchical ideology" should be a bright red warning light to anybody with a clue, because whenever somebody starts going on about that, what they mean is that they don't want any formal hierarchy interfering with the covert hierarchy that gives them their power. (A formal hierarchy normally evolves checks and balances, while a covert hierarchy has none -- this is why consensus schemes so reliably turn abusive and autocratic.) You're right, though, that it does sound like LaSota had either a deliberate devotion to Lucifer (or Satan) or a strong semiconscious attraction toward that.

But talking about trees and forest - In the past decades, thick fir "forests" where fir trees are standing closely together in "neat" rows had been planted. These patches stretch from a few to several 10 acres in size and alternate with "ordinary" forest patches with all kinds of trees. The fir patches have a very artificial, dead vibe resulting from the strange arrangement of the trees but also from the fact that almost no light touches the ground and that, as a result, there is almost no other life. During the last few dry and warm years, the bark beetle has almost completely destroyed those fir patches. After the trees died, they were usually harvested by huge machines and tons over tons of timber are currently being removed from the forests and sold to somewhere.

Somehow it was clear that those fir patches had to die this way over short or long and many have predicted this. The sad thing, however, is that the remaining patches are usually completely empty and devoid of life. If you are lucky, trees from neighboring patches or a few firs have seeded into the fir patches and at least at the fringes saplings are already growing. But other than that - emptiness.

On the other hand, I have the feeling that it does make sense to help these patches recover to forest again and speed the process up a little bit. 2) First and foremost, do divination to see if that's something that should happen. It may be that the land needs to be fallow for a time. If not, yes, both natural and ceremonial magic can be used for this. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Howdy,

If I may add my two cents:

1) Trees are pretty sacred to the Heathen Gods generally, and as far as I know, there's not one "God of the Forest" like Silvanus or the like. Oaks are especially sacred to Thor, yews and ashes are associated with Odin, and apples, of course, with Idun. Jordh is the earth, and so might be appropriate for "growing things/ecosystems" more generally, and both Frey and Freyja are associated with plant growth, but especially with agriculture rather than wild growth, so maybe more appropriate if you planted fruit or nut trees than those meant to be left more-or-less wild. One option, if you want a mix of trees, might be to find particular associations for different Gods/Goddesses and plant one (or more) of each as a sacred grove.

3) The "Miyawaki Method" was developed by a Japanese botanist/ecologist named Akira Miyawaki to re-forest bare ground quickly and in a way that fosters robust, complex natural ecosystems. I haven't been able to find (with only minimal searching, to be fair) a "how to" meant for the do-it-yourself-er, but the basic approach seems to be planting a lot of very young saplings rather densely, of multiple species native/well-adapted to the area, all mixed together, and then mostly let nature take its course. There are groups in some countries that try to apply the method wherever they are, so maybe there's one in your neck of the woods (pun slightly intended). Here's a website of one such group that explains at least at a high level what the method is and why they think it's a good idea: https://www.creatingtomorrowsforests.co.uk/blog/the-miyawaki-method-for-creating-forests

I don't have any direct experience with the method, but it jives with what little I know about ecology and re-wilding and the like, so it intrigues me, despite not having anywhere under my responsibility to get a forest going. If you do end up drawing on this, I'd be very interested to hear how it goes.

Cheers,
Jeff

Bright were the halls then, many the bath-houses,

comments.title: Interaction between Karma and curses
comments.content:
Hi John,

I've read a bit of Hindu mythology lately and am curious as to how a curses work as they relate to karma.

Since one's karma is basically self-created, if somebody got cursed, can the curse do any more than bring up one's own negative karma (and would the curse eventually get removed if all of that negative karma is exhausted)? Taken to an extreme, if somebody tried to curse an absolutely pure being, could that curse have any negative effect whatsoever?

I realize that many mythological stories simply use curses as plot devices, but I am just curious how you would view "real life" consequences.

replies.title: Re: Interaction between Karma and curses
replies.content:
There are two kinds of curses. Let me quote an old blog post of mine:

"It occurs to me that some of my readers may not be familiar with the structure and function of curses. (What do they teach children these days?) The sort of thing you get in bad modern remakes of fairy tales, where someone inoffensive gets burdened with a dire fate that would not otherwise befall them, is strictly amateur stuff. Professionals know that the curses that matter are the kind that unfold by their own inexorable logic from the actions and attitudes of the accursed. The witch or wizard who finds it necessary or appropriate to pronounce a curse doesn’t have to make anything happen; he or she simply says aloud the unmentionable realities of the situation, states the necessary consequences, and leaves. The efforts of the accursed to avoid falling victim to the curse, without actually changing the things that make the curse inevitable, then proceed to drive the curse to its fulfillment."

The first, or amateur-hour variety of curse, is an act of violence inflicted by the curser on the victim. It can be temporarily effective, but it relieves the victim of some bad karma while piling a good hefty helping of bad karma on the curser. The second, or professional variety of curse, is karmically neutral. The curser here simply states the realities of the situation and lets them work out. Mythology tends to be a mix of both kinds.
